prompt: Japanese Edo-period ukiyo-e woodblock print in the style of Katsushika Hokusai circa 1830. A lone traveler in traditional blue-grey robes descends a dramatic mountainous landscape, following a winding path that visibly traces the steepest available slope at each step — the path zigzags downward through several ridges and valleys before reaching the flat plain below. The mountain rendered entirely in flat, unmodulated areas of Prussian blue and ochre with bold black ink outlines defining every contour. No shading, no photographic gradients, no Western-style form modeling. Pure flat color fills separated by crisp outline. Dramatic diagonal composition from upper-left peak descending to lower-right valley floor. Atmospheric bokashi sky gradation from deep indigo at top to pale ivory at horizon. Visible woodgrain texture across the print surface. Style strictly faithful to Hokusai's Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji series. No text, no labels, no diagrams.
tradition_approach: Flat unmodulated color fills, bold black ink outlines, Prussian blue and ochre palette, diagonal compositional thrust, atmospheric bokashi sky gradation, and visible woodgrain texture targeting Hokusai's Thirty-six Views grammar.
